markdown:
## Rapid Prototyping: Accelerating Design Development


<definition term="Rapid Prototyping">Rapid prototyping is the use of **3D printing and other additive techniques **to quickly create physical models from CAD designs, allowing fast testing and feedback.</definition>

1. It is **often referred too as 3D printing**, especially in schools and industry, but technically, **3D printing is just one part **of rapid prototyping.
2. Rapid prototyping focuses on **speed, iteration, and development**, not just the printing itself.
3. All **3D printing methods** (FDM, SLS and SLA) can be used for rapid prototyping, depending on the detail, speed, and material needed. 

#### Why Designers Use Rapid Prototyping


1. Produces prototypes **quickly **from CAD
2. Enables **frequent iteration **and fast feedback
3. Ideal for **complex or customised designs**
4. **No tooling or moulds **required

![Rapid prototyping used in design development ](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/52f5ab22c4748a6ca2c8f55035cd4e1ac1aae270-2500x1664.png)

### Key Rapid Prototyping Techniques


#### Stereolithography (SLA)


1. **Stereolithography (SLA)** uses a **laser** to cure **liquid resin** into solid layers, building a 3D model.
2. Produces **very high-detail **and **smooth surface finishes**, ideal for **aesthetic models**.
3. Commonly used for **small, intricate parts **like dental models, jewellery, or concept casings.
4. Requires **post-processing**, such as **washing **and **UV curing**, after printing. 

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
| --- | --- |
| High precision and detail  | Weaker mechanical properties - more brittle than FDM or SLS |
| Wide range of resins available | Post-processing required - messy and time-consuming |
| Excellent surface finish | Higher cost per part - maintenance and resin is expensive  |

![Stereolithography used to create a part with intricate detail](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/c41eba9955307be57ae9cc67ac5635994b96a609-1280x720.png)

#### Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)


1. **FDM involves extruding thermoplastic filament** through a heated nozzle, building the model layer by layer.
2. Common in schools and hobbyist settings due to **low cost and simplicity**.
3. Works best for **basic prototypes**, functional parts, and enclosures.
4. Requires **support structures **for overhangs, which must be removed after printing. 
5. Common filament materials include ABS and PLA.

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
| --- | --- |
| Low cost & widely available | Visible layer lines may lead to poor surface finish |
| Good for functional parts | Limited detail on small features |
| Easy setup & operation | May have weaker points along the layer lines, affecting structural integrity |

![Fused deposition modeling of a gear mechanism](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/7a4bc9d57541fd1534fd780deb36a659ff2d0511-1616x1080.png)

#### Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)


1. SLS uses a **laser** to fuse **powdered material** (such as nylon or metal) into solid layers, creating a 3D model.
2. Ideal for **complex parts**, including **internal features **and **moving mechanisms**.
3. No need for support structures, surrounding powder holds the print in place.
4. Often used in **engineering, medical, and industrial design **contexts.

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
| --- | --- |
| Strong and durable prototypes | High equipment and material cost |
| No support structures needed | Requires post-processing (depowdering) |
| Prints complex shapes with ease | Surface can be slightly grainy |

![Schematic of a SLS printer](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/71672c1f002b038368d9c228a2898eeb0cdf3e11-1200x675.png)

<callout type="analogy">* Think of SLS like building a sandcastle. 
* The laser acts like a sculptor, fusing the grains of sand (powder) into a solid structure, while the surrounding sand supports the shape until it's complete.</callout>

<inlineVideo caption="Comparing SLA, FDM & SLS" />

<callout type="note">* Rapid prototyping is not limited to physical models.
* It can also include digital simulations and virtual reality environments, enabling early and efficient testing of design functionality, usability, and user interaction.</callout>

<example_question>
        <question>
        An architecture studio uses stereolithography (SLA) to produce a detailed scale model of a new building, layer by layer cured in photopolymer resin.

**Which best states the manufacturing process used to create this scale model?**

  1. Subtractive
  2. Additive
  3. Joining
  4. Finishing
        </question>
        <answer>
        Award 1 mark for the correct answer: b - additive
        </answer>
      </example_question>
